dialogue.utterance: 911, this is Dispatch. Unit 3 for a sick person with abdominal pain at Richmond address.
Hi, I'm Mike, a paramedic with the rescue squad. My partner is Sarah. What made you call 911 today?
We called because his stomach has been hurting. He started saying his chest hurts too.
Yeah, my stomach is hurting all over. My chest feels very sharp and constant.
Mr. Jenkins, what is the day of the week?
It's Wednesday, but I'm not sure of the date.
What county are we in?
Richmond County, I think.
What brought you to call 911 today?
This pain all over the abdomen. And the chest feels like there's a knife in there. I've been weak and can barely move.
Mr. Jenkins, I'll check your airway now. Can you stay still?
Yeah.
Your airway is open and clear. No signs of obstruction.
Sarah, let's get the respiratory rate and quality.
Respiratory rate is 16. Normal chest rise and fall. No signs of distress.
Mr. Jenkins, you mentioned chest pain earlier. Let me ask a bit more about it.
It's sharp and constant. Hasn't gone away since it started.
Does it radiate anywhere else like your arm, back, or jaw?
No, it stays right straight forward.
Blood pressure is 84 over 53. Slightly low.
Pulse is 48-slow and weak. Skin is warm and dry, but a bit pale. Peripheral pulses are present but weak.
Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
Mr. Jenkins, you mentioned weakness. How are you walking with your cane?
I feel like my legs are week and wobbly. I use the cane to stay steady.
Blood glucose is 104. That's a normal range for you.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive. Around 4 mm in diameter.
Mr. Jenkins, you are alert and oriented to person, place, and time, though not sure of the event.
Head and neck appear normal. No signs of trauma or masses.
He barely knows when it started. He just says it's been all day.
Mr. Jenkins, can you tell me if anything makes the pain better or worse?
Nothing makes it better or worse. It just is there all the time.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how bad is the pain right now?
Eight.
Mr. Jenkins, what medications do you take regularly?
I take something for my diabetes and my heart.
Sarah, let's take a look at the medication blister pack.
Insulin Glargine, Furosemide, Lasix, Amlodipine, Lisinopril, Metformin, Levothyroxine, and Insulin Detemir.
Mr. Jenkins, are you allergic to any medications?
I'm allergic to fentanyl.
EKG is sinus bradycardia. No signs of immediate heart attack yet.
Sarah, let's start the 4-lead ECG monitoring now.
4-lead ECG started. Rhythm is still sinus bradycardia.
Mr. Jenkins, may I listen to your lungs?
Go ahead.
Lungs are clear. No signs of effusion or crackles.
Are you having any chest discomfort now?
Yes. The sharp one again.
Let me palpate your abdomen. It feels soft and not tender.
Your skin appears normal. Pupils are reactive. No changes in color or temperature.
Bystander, does he have a history of heart failure?
Yes, he has CHF and high blood pressure as well.
Sarah, let's get an IV started in his left hand. Flush with normal saline.
IV inserted at 20G in left hand. Flush is on.
12-lead EKG obtained. Shows sinus bradycardia with multiple arrhythmias. Indicates potential myocardial infarct.
Mr. Jenkins, where would you like to be taken? Chippenham is the suggestion we have.
Chippenham is fine with me. I know them well.
Family member is welcome to come with you. Just let me know.
I can come if that's okay with the EMT.
That's fine. Mr. Jenkins is walking with your help. Loading into the ambulance now.
Assistance from the family and ambulatory movement for transfer. Stretcher moved and secured.
Fire door closed and seat belts secured. Air ambulance mode activated.
Pulse is 43. Still weak but no worsening.
Respirations remain at 15. No changes.
Blood pressure is 75 over 51.
Glucose is still 104.
Patient now transferred via sheet draw to hospital bed with assistance from hospital staff.
From Mike and Sarah. Total fluids: 200cc normal saline administered. IV remains patent.
12-lead EKG obtained showing possible inferior infarct and bradycardia with arrhythmias.
Mr. Jenkins has persistent chest pain at level eight, persistent weakness, and reported having diarrhea this evening.
Blood pressure and heart rate remain low, no improvement with fluids. No interventions performed en route.
Patient is alert and oriented to person, place, and time, though history is limited.
Reports generalized abdominal discomfort with no localized findings, and no trauma or rashes found.
Blood pressure is 76/48 now. That matches the last known stable reading. Condition has not changed.
No signs of urination issues or rash reported in the field.
He is capable of answering questions and providing adequate history, but is confused during more complex questioning.
Mr. Jenkins remains A/O x4: alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event upon arrival.
He expresses his health concerns clearly and is not reporting anything out of the ordinary beyond the symptoms we have.
No signs of internal or external trauma noted on skin or body. No diagnosed chronic health issues under direct review.
EKG deviation at rest suggests possible cardiac anomalies but no signs of respiratory distress.
Patient has a known history of umbilical hernia and diabetes, but no acute symptoms from those now.
No stabilization needed beyond IV and monitoring. No prevention or pain management attempted during transport.
Transport was smooth with no emergency scenarios or active deterioration observed en route.
Mr. Jenkins is now handed over for emergency room assessment at Chippenham Hospital ER Room 20.
Bystander is transferred alongside. So, we have no open patients or other care needs at this time.
Blood pressure remains low, pulse continued with weak readings, no signs of respiratory crisis or sudden drop in consciousness.
No active interventions or resuscitation performed. Protocol for medical abdominal pain is the primary course.
Skin turgor, temperature, and hydration are normal. No abnormal vital changes reported.
Mr. Jenkins remains under consideration for emergent care given hypotension and bradycardia signs.
Last glucose is normal, blood sugar is stable overall, with typical diabetic medications.
Only the family can help provide medical background due to limited patient recall and confusion.
The patient reported fecal incontinence. No signs of internal bleeding or obstruction found.
Transport time and route were uneventful. Arrived at the destination with the patient under full EMT care.
Hospital care transitioned with full administrative and medical signatures on record.
Mr. Jenkins is in stable condition at this time, under the care of ED medical staff.
No prevention or post-care instructions requested now. We are out for further calls. Have a good one.
